title: Kedgeree
subtitle: Smoked haddock with spiced rice and soft-boiled eggs
excerpt: Flaked smoked haddock folded through lightly curried basmati rice with butter, soft-boiled eggs cut in quarters, and chopped parsley. A British-Indian dish of the Raj period, originally a spiced lentil-and-rice breakfast dish from Bengal transformed into a smoked fish brunch by the Anglo-Indian household. Warm, restrained spicing — turmeric and mild curry powder rather than chilli heat.
sourceNotes: Kedgeree derives from the Indian khichri (spiced rice and lentils), adapted by the British in India during the Raj as a breakfast or brunch dish using smoked or fresh fish. Documented in Mrs Beeton's Household Management (1861) and British-Indian cookbooks throughout the 20th century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the rice and poach the fish ahead. Combine and heat through to serve.

content.text: Smoked haddock is the correct fish for kedgeree — not salmon, not fresh haddock, not any other substitution. The smoke flavour is structural to the dish. Natural smoked haddock (pale, not the dyed yellow variety) has a more restrained smoke flavour that integrates better with the lightly spiced rice.

The spicing in kedgeree is gentle by comparison with Indian restaurant dishes: turmeric for colour, a small amount of curry powder or cumin and coriander for warmth, and butter rather than oil as the fat. The rice should look yellow from the turmeric but taste mild. The smoked fish is the dominant flavour; the spices are support.
Cook the rice and poach the fish

Cook the {{basmati-rice}} by the absorption method: 1 part rice to 1.5 parts water, covered, 12 minutes on the lowest heat, then rested 10 minutes. Meanwhile, place the {{smoked-haddock}} in a wide pan, cover with cold water, and bring to a gentle simmer. Poach for 6 to 8 minutes until the fish flakes easily. Remove and cool slightly, then remove the skin and flake the fish into large pieces, checking for any bones. Reserve 60 ml of the poaching liquid.

Soft-boil the {{eggs}}: lower into boiling water, cook for 6 minutes, then cool in cold water before peeling and halving. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes until soft but not coloured. Add the {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and stir for 30 seconds. Add the cooked rice and the reserved poaching liquid (or {{double-cream}} if using) and stir gently to combine. Fold in the flaked fish. Season with salt and {{black-pepper}}. Arrange the egg halves on top, scatter with {{flat-leaf-parsley}}, and serve with lemon wedges.

items.symptom: Fish breaks into tiny flakes rather than large pieces
items.cause: Overcooked or broken up too vigorously
items.fix: Poach gently until just flaking and fold the fish in with minimal stirring. Large flakes give the dish texture; dust does not

items.cause: Smoked haddock is already salty — salt was added on top
items.fix: Do not add salt until tasting at the end. The fish and the poaching liquid will season the rice sufficiently in most cases

items.cause: Absorption method produced stiff rice, or cream/liquid not added
items.fix: Add a generous splash of the fish poaching liquid when folding in the rice. It should be moist but not wet
6 minutes in boiling water, then immediately into cold water. The yolk should be just set at the edges with a moist centre

content.text: Kedgeree occupies a distinct position in British cooking: it is the primary contribution of Anglo-Indian domestic cooking to the mainstream British repertoire. The Raj household adapted an Indian dish into a breakfast and brunch staple, substituting smoked fish for the lentils and reducing the spice to suit early-morning eating. It sits alongside other Anglo-Indian inheritances (mulligatawny soup, coronation chicken) as evidence of the culinary exchange between British and Indian cooking during the 19th century. In modern British cooking it appears most often as a weekend brunch dish.
